title: Mee Goreng

overview.description: A Malaysian-style fried noodle dish with egg noodles, chicken, vegetables, egg and a rich, spicy-sweet sauce.

ingredients: 300g boneless chicken thighs, cut into thin strips
1 tbsp sambal oelek or other chilli paste
method: Prepare the egg noodles according to the packet instructions, taking care not to overcook them.
Drain thoroughly and set aside.
Mix all the sauce ingredients together in a small bowl.
Heat a wok or large frying pan over high heat.
Add 1 tbsp of the oil.
Add the chicken and stir-fry until cooked through and lightly browned.
Remove the chicken and set aside.
Add the remaining oil to the wok.
Add the garlic, shallots and onion.
Stir-fry until fragrant and lightly softened.
Push the aromatics to one side of the wok.
Crack in the eggs and scramble them until just set.
Add the cabbage and stir-fry briefly.
Return the chicken to the wok.
Add the noodles and sauce.
Toss everything together over high heat until the noodles are evenly coated and hot.
Add the tomato, bean sprouts and spring onions.
Stir-fry briefly so the vegetables remain slightly crisp.
Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Serve immediately with lime wedges.

learning.did-you-know: Mee goreng means fried noodles, but the Malaysian mamak version is particularly associated with Indian Muslim food stalls and restaurants, where it developed its distinctive combination of chilli, tomato and sweet soy flavours.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to cook a complete noodle dish over high heat while balancing sweet, salty, savoury and spicy flavours.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Malaysian noodle dish that gives the egg noodles a completely different identity from chow mein through its spicy, sweet and tomato-rich sauce.
chapman-tips: Prepare every ingredient before starting; the cooking process is very fast once the wok is hot.
Don't overcook the noodles before they go into the wok.
If using dried egg noodles, rinse them briefly with cold water after cooking to stop them becoming too soft.
Kecap manis provides sweetness, colour and a distinctive Indonesian-Malaysian flavour.
The tomato should be added near the end so it softens without completely disappearing into the sauce.
Bean sprouts are best added right at the end so they retain some crunch.
Keep the heat high and avoid overcrowding the wok.
Adjust the sambal to control heat separately from the other flavours.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The noodles can become soft and the vegetables lose their texture when frozen and reheated.
notes.leftovers: Reheat quickly in a hot wok or frying pan. Leftover noodles can also be used as a base for a quick fried noodle lunch.
